case.note: Case report PMC12695512 / PMID 40984660. 38-year-old man with allergic rhinitis experienced seven episodes of anaphylaxis at home, one progressing to anaphylactic shock. He denied medication use, specific foods, or Hymenoptera stings. Reactions coincided with periods when his children were receiving penicillin therapy. Two episodes had identified triggers (Table 1): licking child's spoon with amoxicillin syrup traces (5 min onset), and drinking from child's glass containing traces of a crushed amoxicillin tablet (10 min onset).

medications.episode: Recurrent idiopathic anaphylaxis / allergy by proxy to penicillins within the household
medications.source_span: Licking his child's spoon containing traces of amoxicillin syrup

allergies: Positive serum-specific IgE for amoxicillin (0.26 kUA/L)
Skin prick test positive to amoxicillin (0.2 mg/mL; 1:100)
Skin prick test positive to ampicillin (2 mg/mL; 1:10)
Intradermal test positive to penicillin G (10,000 IU/mL; undiluted)
No hypersensitivity to cefuroxime and ceftriaxone (negative skin tests and drug provocation)
diagnoses: Allergy by proxy to penicillins presenting as recurrent idiopathic anaphylaxis within the household
Seven episodes of anaphylaxis at home, one progressing to anaphylactic shock

ambiguities.question: The amoxicillin exposure is documented as indirect (trace contact via a child's glass with a crushed amoxicillin tablet). Please confirm this should be classified as indirect-exposure rather than a patient medication.

A second indirect amoxicillin trigger (licking child's spoon with amoxicillin syrup traces, 5 min onset) is described in Table 1 but was not among the provided source strings. Should this separate indirect exposure also be recorded?

intake.sourceSummary: Single case report (PMC12695512) of a 38-year-old man with recurrent anaphylaxis caused by indirect (by-proxy) exposure to penicillins from his children's therapy. Provided source string documents drinking from a child's glass with traces of a crushed amoxicillin tablet. Tryptase and amoxicillin-specific IgE values preserved verbatim.

patient.note: Case report PMC12695512 / PMID 40984660. 38-year-old man with allergic rhinitis experienced seven episodes of anaphylaxis at home, one progressing to anaphylactic shock. He denied medication use, specific foods, or Hymenoptera stings. Reactions coincided with periods when his children were receiving penicillin therapy. Two episodes had identified triggers (Table 1): licking child's spoon with amoxicillin syrup traces (5 min onset), and drinking from child's glass containing traces of a crushed amoxicillin tablet (10 min onset).

report.patient_summary: 38-year-old man with allergic rhinitis and documented sensitisation to penicillins. He reports seven episodes of anaphylaxis at home, one progressing to anaphylactic shock, with two episodes linked to trace exposure to amoxicillin (licking a child's spoon with amoxicillin syrup traces, onset 5 minutes; drinking from a child's glass with traces of a crushed amoxicillin tablet, onset 10 minutes). Documented allergy testing shows positive serum-specific IgE for amoxicillin (0.26 kUA/L), positive skin prick tests to amoxicillin and ampicillin, and positive intradermal test to penicillin G, with no hypersensitivity to cefuroxime and ceftriaxone. Baseline serum tryptase is 4.1 μg/L. The only medication entry is amoxicillin recorded as an indirect environmental exposure, not an active order.
questions_for_clinician: The amoxicillin entry is recorded as an indirect environmental exposure rather than a prescribed medication — can you confirm whether the patient is intended to be actively taking any medication, or whether this entry reflects only trace household exposure?
No interaction or safety evidence was supplied for review; are there additional active medications you would like screened?
